Curriculum → AI Engineer learning path → Machine learning foundations → Model evaluation and validation

Lesson 3 of 3

Model evaluation and validation

Cross-validation, metrics selection, overfitting detection, and experiment tracking best practices.

25-35 min AI/ML flow Machine learning foundations 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

A model is only as good as its evaluation. Proper validation prevents costly production failures and builds trust in ML systems.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Validation strategies K-fold cross-validation gives robust estimates on small datasets2. Metrics that matter Accuracy misleads on imbalanced classes; prefer precision-recall analysis3. Detecting and preventing overfitting A widening gap between train and validation loss signals overfitting

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing A model is only as good as its evaluation. Proper validation prevents costly production failures and builds trust in ML systems. 2. Validation strategies K-fold cross-validation gives robust estimates on small datasets3. Metrics that matter Accuracy misleads on imbalanced classes; prefer precision-recall analysis4. Detecting and preventing overfitting A widening gap between train and validation loss signals overfitting
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

A model is only as good as its evaluation. Proper validation prevents costly production failures and builds trust in ML systems.

What to say first

  • Can choose the right validation strategy for the data distribution.
  • Selects metrics aligned with business objectives rather than defaulting to accuracy.
  • Monitors learning curves and applies regularization when overfitting appears.
2

Study section

Validation strategies

Hold-out splits, k-fold cross-validation, stratified sampling, and time-series splits each suit different data distributions.

Call these points out explicitly

  • K-fold cross-validation gives robust estimates on small datasets
  • Stratified splits preserve class ratios in imbalanced problems
  • Time-series data requires chronological splits to avoid look-ahead bias
3

Study section

Metrics that matter

Accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUC-ROC, RMSE, and MAE each tell a different story about model quality.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Accuracy misleads on imbalanced classes; prefer precision-recall analysis
  • AUC-ROC measures ranking quality independent of threshold
  • Regression metrics (RMSE, MAE) differ in sensitivity to outliers
4

Study section

Detecting and preventing overfitting

Learning curves, regularization, early stopping, and data augmentation keep models generalizable.

Call these points out explicitly

  • A widening gap between train and validation loss signals overfitting
  • Regularization (L1/L2, dropout) penalizes model complexity
  • More data and augmentation are often the most effective remedies

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Can choose the right validation strategy for the data distribution.
  • Selects metrics aligned with business objectives rather than defaulting to accuracy.
  • Monitors learning curves and applies regularization when overfitting appears.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Optimizing accuracy on an imbalanced dataset without examining per-class performance.
  • Leaking future information into training data in time-series problems.
  • Tuning hyperparameters on the test set instead of a separate validation fold.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • How would you evaluate a fraud detection model when only 0.1% of transactions are fraudulent?
  • What does a ROC curve tell you that accuracy alone does not?
  • Describe a scenario where cross-validation is misleading.

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Explain the concept

Teach "Model evaluation and validation" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Guardrails

  • How would you evaluate a fraud detection model when only 0.1% of transactions are fraudulent?
  • Validation strategies
  • Metrics that matter
  • Can choose the right validation strategy for the data distribution.

Suggested Structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs
